items.question: What is data augmentation and why is it used?
items.answer: Data augmentation applies label-preserving transforms to training examples — image flips/crops, text paraphrase, tabular noise or SMOTE — to increase input diversity without new labels. It acts as regularization against overfitting when labeled data is limited and the model memorizes training specifics.

items.question: How does augmentation differ from L2 regularization or early stopping?
items.answer: Augmentation diversifies training inputs while keeping labels fixed. L2 penalizes large weights; early stopping halts training when validation error worsens. All three reduce overfitting but through different mechanisms and are often combined.

items.question: Why should validation data not use random augmentation?
items.answer: Validation estimates performance on stable, deployment-like inputs. Random augmentation adds noise to metrics and can hide the true generalization gap. Use deterministic preprocessing on val/test; reserve random transforms for training only.

items.question: Name three image transforms and when they are inappropriate.
items.answer: Common transforms: horizontal flip, random crop, color jitter. They are inappropriate when the label depends on orientation (left vs right), exact crop content (pixel-level segmentation with wrong masks), or color being the signal (stain-based medical labels).

items.question: What is SMOTE and when might you mention it alongside augmentation?
items.answer: SMOTE synthesizes minority-class tabular rows by interpolating between neighbors in feature space. Use it when class imbalance causes the model to overfit the majority head — apply only on training data after split, not on validation or test.

items.question: When will augmentation fail to help?
items.answer: When both training and validation error are high (underfitting), when transforms break label semantics, when deployment shift is not covered by any transform, or when label noise dominates — augmentation increases variance coverage but cannot fix wrong labels or missing signal.
